title: 1. Docker Introduction
explanation: Docker is a tool that packages applications into isolated containers. Each container bundles the app code, runtime, and all system libraries — so it runs identically on any machine. Run docker --version to confirm the client is installed.
instruction: Check the Docker version installed on this sandbox system.

title: 2. Docker Architecture
explanation: Docker uses a client-server model. The Docker client (CLI) sends commands to the Docker daemon (dockerd), which builds, runs, and manages containers. Run docker info to see the daemon's live system information.
instruction: Query the Docker daemon for system-wide configuration details.
expected: Kernel version, OS, and container count are displayed.

title: 3. Your First Container
explanation: The docker run command creates and starts a container from an image. The hello-world image is Docker's official 'it works!' test. When you run it, Docker pulls the image and prints a success message.
instruction: Run the hello-world test container to verify Docker is working.
expected: 'Hello from Docker!' success message is printed.
hint: Run exact command: docker run hello-world

title: 4. Pulling Images
explanation: Images are like blueprints for containers. They are stored on Docker Hub (registry). Use docker pull to download an image locally. docker images shows all images you have downloaded.
instruction: Pull the lightweight Alpine Linux image from Docker Hub.
expected: alpine image downloaded and listed under docker images.
hint: Run exact command: docker pull alpine

title: 5. Running a Container
explanation: The -d flag runs the container in the background (detached). The --name flag assigns a human-readable name. Here we launch Nginx, a popular web server, as a background container.
instruction: Launch an Nginx web server container named my_web running in the background.
expected: A long container hex ID is printed and my_web is running.
hint: Run exact command: docker run -d --name my_web nginx

title: 6. Listing Containers
explanation: docker ps shows running containers only. Adding the -a flag shows ALL containers — including stopped ones like hello-world. This gives you the full container history.
instruction: List all containers including stopped ones in the sandbox.
expected: Table showing my_web (running) and hello-world (exited) containers.

title: 7. Writing a Dockerfile
explanation: A Dockerfile is a text file with step-by-step instructions to build a custom Docker image. Each line is a command: FROM sets the base image, RUN executes shell commands inside the image during build. Create a minimal Dockerfile using the echo command.
example: echo -e "FROM alpine\nRUN echo 'Hello from my image!'" > Dockerfile
instruction: Create a Dockerfile in the current directory with FROM alpine and a RUN instruction.
expected: Dockerfile file exists. Use cat Dockerfile to verify its contents.
hint: Run exact command: echo -e "FROM alpine\nRUN echo 'Hello from my image!'" > Dockerfile

title: 8. Building an Image
explanation: docker build reads the Dockerfile and assembles an image layer by layer. The -t flag gives the image a name and tag (name:version). The dot (.) at the end means 'use the Dockerfile in the current directory'.
instruction: Build a Docker image tagged custom_app:v1 from your Dockerfile.
hint: Run exact command: docker build -t custom_app:v1 .

title: 9. Layer Caching
explanation: Docker caches each build step. If you rebuild the same image without changing the Dockerfile, Docker skips unchanged steps using cached layers. This makes rebuilds nearly instant. Try building the same image again!
instruction: Run the exact same build command again to observe the 'Using cache' speed.
expected: Build shows 'Using cache' on all steps and completes immediately.
hint: Run exact command: docker build -t custom_app:v1 .

title: 10. Volumes
explanation: Containers are ephemeral — when they stop, their data is lost. Docker Volumes are persistent storage that live independently of containers. Create a named volume to store data that survives container restarts.
instruction: Create a Docker volume named db_data for persistent database storage.
expected: db_data is printed (volume name confirmed). Run docker volume ls to see it.
hint: Run exact command: docker volume create db_data

title: 11. Networks
explanation: By default, containers run in isolation. Docker Networks let containers communicate securely by name. User-defined bridge networks provide automatic DNS — containers find each other using their names, not IP addresses.
instruction: Create a custom bridge network named backend_net for container communication.
expected: Network ID printed. Run docker network ls to see backend_net listed.
hint: Run exact command: docker network create backend_net

title: 12. Environment Variables
explanation: Applications need configuration (ports, API keys, etc.). Docker passes environment variables into containers using the -e flag. These become available as shell variables inside the container at runtime.
instruction: Run a detached Nginx container named env_web with PORT set to 8080 using -e.
expected: env_web container started. Run docker ps to confirm it is running.
hint: Run exact command: docker run -d --name env_web -e PORT=8080 nginx

title: 13. Viewing Logs
explanation: Containers run processes in the background. Use docker logs to see the standard output (stdout/stderr) from any container — great for debugging startup errors or checking service activity.
instruction: Fetch and display the startup logs from the my_web Nginx container.
expected: Nginx startup log lines are printed to the terminal.
hint: Run exact command: docker logs my_web

title: 14. Exec Into a Container
explanation: docker exec runs a command inside a running container. You can use it to debug, check files, or run diagnostic commands without stopping the container. Try running the date command inside my_web.
instruction: Run the 'date' command inside the my_web container using docker exec.
expected: Current date and time from inside the container is printed.
hint: Run exact command: docker exec my_web date

title: 15. Inspecting Resources
explanation: docker inspect returns detailed JSON metadata about any container, image, volume, or network. This is useful for debugging networking issues, checking IP addresses, mount paths, and environment settings.
instruction: Inspect the default bridge network to see its full JSON configuration.
expected: A JSON array with network configuration fields is displayed.
hint: Run exact command: docker inspect bridge

title: 16. Multi-stage Builds
explanation: Multi-stage builds use multiple FROM blocks. You compile code in a large 'builder' stage, then copy only the output binary into a minimal final image. This dramatically reduces image size for production deployments.
example:
echo -e "FROM alpine AS builder
RUN touch /app
FROM alpine
COPY --from=builder /app /app" > Dockerfile.multi
instruction: Create a Dockerfile.multi file demonstrating a multi-stage build with two FROM stages.
expected: Dockerfile.multi exists with FROM alpine AS builder and COPY --from=builder instructions.
hint:
Run exact command: echo -e "FROM alpine AS builder
RUN touch /app
FROM alpine
COPY --from=builder /app /app" > Dockerfile.multi

title: 17. Stop & Remove Containers
explanation: Running containers use CPU and memory. docker stop sends a graceful shutdown signal. docker rm deletes the stopped container record. Always clean up containers you no longer need to free resources.
example: docker stop my_web && docker rm my_web
instruction: Stop the my_web container and then remove it to free resources.
expected: my_web is stopped and removed. It no longer appears in docker ps -a.
hint: Run exact command: docker stop my_web && docker rm my_web

title: 18. Docker Compose
explanation: docker-compose.yml defines your entire multi-container application in one file. Instead of running multiple docker run commands, Compose starts, links, and configures all services together with one command.
example:
echo -e "services:
  web:
    image: nginx
  db:
    image: redis" > docker-compose.yml
instruction: Create a docker-compose.yml file with a web (nginx) and db (redis) service.
expected: docker-compose.yml exists. Use cat docker-compose.yml to read the file contents.
hint:
Run exact command: echo -e "services:
  web:
    image: nginx
  db:
    image: redis" > docker-compose.yml
